name: Nefertem
description: Nefertem, in Egyptian mythology, is the god of perfume, healing, and beauty. He is depicted as a beautiful young man, often wearing a headdress of blue water lilies or a lion's head. As the god of perfume, he embodies the sweet fragrance of the lotus flower, which was believed to have emerged from the primordial waters of Nun. Nefertem's origins are closely tied to the sun god Ra; some accounts claim he was born from a lotus flower that blossomed on the nose of Ra, while others state that he is the son of Bastet or Sekhmet. He is associated with healing and rejuvenation, often depicted carrying healing herbs. His role extends to protecting the innocent and bringing joy to the world, symbolizing the promise of rebirth and renewal. Nefertem is closely linked to the concept of the rising sun, representing the dawn of a new day and the blossoming of life. He is considered a gentle and benevolent deity, offering solace and hope to those who seek his aid. He is a powerful symbol of the beauty and fragrance that can arise even from the darkest of times, representing the transformative power of beauty and the enduring promise of healing.
subtitle: God of Perfume, Healing, and Beauty

keyMyths.title: Birth from a Lotus
keyMyths.description: Nefertem is said to have been born from a lotus flower that emerged from the primordial waters, symbolizing creation and beauty. This birth is often linked to Ra, the sun god.

keyMyths.title: Son of Bastet/Sekhmet
keyMyths.description: In some versions, Nefertem is considered the son of either Bastet or Sekhmet, linking him to the protective and healing aspects of these powerful goddesses. This connection reinforces his role as a benevolent deity.

keyMyths.title: The Healing Fragrance
keyMyths.description: Nefertem's association with perfume represents the healing power of fragrance and the ability of scents to soothe the soul and bring about rejuvenation. He carries healing herbs to offer solace.

sources.author: Richard H. Wilkinson

sources.author: Manly P. Hall
